At no point did we ever suggest that this was a brand marketing course like it? Itĺs not the momentum builder workshop is designed to solve a very specific problem, which is to prevent procrastination. Itĺs really easy to procrastinate across two dimensions. One is I donĺt know what I should be doing. And then the other is, I know what I should be doing. But I donĺt know how to do it. Like those are two dimensions of procrastination. And thatĺs the problem. That momentum builder workshop was was designed to solve where itĺs like, letĺs figure out the shortest path is not exactly the shortest path, but from our perspective, the shortest viable path between a prospective customer and a potential sale. Right. Now, we could arguably say itĺs not the shortest path, because we could just go add to sales page, right? Thatĺs the shortest path. And thatĺs fine, but thatĺs not the momentum builder workshop. It doesnĺt line up with the things that we teach. So weĺre Were the idea of the workshop is to say, how do we connect an audience with an offer in the shortest number of steps that we feel good about the way that we teach. And that eliminates the two dimensions of procrastination that are so common, because when you, we make it very clear, these are the things that you need to do. And then within each of those things, we say, this is how you do it. Right? And theyĺre not quite sure what they need to do to do to do that. And then when they realize what they need to do, theyĺre not quite sure how to do it. I would encourage everyone listening to this, to ask yourself, Why am I looking outside of the scope of what Iĺm focused on? In my day? Am I concerned? Am I procrastinating? Do I not? But this is thereĺs thereĺs a funny question that gets asked in and Iĺm sure this is true. Well beyond internet marketing circles, but in our corner of the world, thereĺs a funny little switch with questions that happens in hereĺs what the hereĺs the switch. Most people ask the question, particularly agencies, this is the if you ever run an agency, this is the the agency trick. When a company is working with an agency, another company comes in, and they show the company that theyĺre that theyĺre trying to attract. They put together these massive reports of everything that they could be doing. I could thatĺs the key word. So you know, you could be got blogging, you could be podcasting. You could be doing video, and youĺre not doing any of these things. So the agency youĺre working with is they canĺt be very good because you could be doing so much that youĺre not doing right. The problem with could is it assumes that time Energy in dollars are infinite. In that case, there are millions of things that we could do. But in the real world, time, energy and dollars are finite, which means that we have to ask the question should versus could. So, given the given the amount of time, energy and dollars I have to spend, and the goal that Iĺm trying to achieve, what should I do? Well, that eliminate 99.9 of your charts presented your choices immediately. Because if youĺre just getting started, which is really the focus of bringing an idea out into the world, thatĺs the focus of the momentum builder workshop, if youĺre, if youĺre just getting started in trying to make contact between an audience and an offer, podcasting probably is not your best option. the reason that was so effective was because of the of the offer. Right? It wasnĺt because of the this is something Andre and I have talked about repeated, there is no causal relationship weĺve ever seen, between an OT the structure of, quote, unquote, a funnel or a business or a system, and the output the result, right. So thereĺs no, I know, this is this is going to shock some of you based on what everybody else is saying on the internet, but thereĺs no way to do what we do. Functionally, structurally, technology technologically, that guarantees a result, thereĺs there just isnĺt. Because at the end of the day, marketing always comes down to two things offer an audience. And if you put the right offer in front of the right audience, everything else, it doesnĺt matter what I mean, you can make a million mistakes, and you still get a good result. If you put the wrong offer in front of the right audience or the right off in front of the wrong audience, you can figure out the different permutations here. It just doesnĺt work. Right. So that leads me to start thinking about the question of how how does the momentum builder workshop connect the right audience to the right offer? And I think thatĺs the better way to think about this, like, does it do that? And how does it do that? And the answer is, yes. It thatĺs how itĺs built. Itĺs built to get the attention of the right audience, meaning weĺre not using curiosity to get clicks, because we want to get, you know, 1000 leads hoping that we have 10 customers in there, weĺre not playing the volume game, the quantity game, weĺre playing the quality game. So immediately, weĺre weĺre able to attract fewer people of higher quality, which, again, if that, depending on what the eventual offer is, higher quality is always better. Thatĺs just I think thatĺs something that we would all agree to that if all things being equal, would you rather have 10 high quality prospects, or 10, low quality prospects? Weĺre all going to say high quality like thatĺs thatĺs thereĺs thereĺs no rationale to say the opposite. Shawn Ś 33:42

interesting, something that you just said hints at something, or points to something really interesting, too. And Iĺd love to draw this Sunday. But I think itĺs a bell curve. Right? And then the vertical axis is, is how much do you have to give in exchange for that? And whatĺs whatĺs interesting? Is it maybe itĺs not a bell curve, I would have I need to I want to spend some time with this. It may be a linear curve, or maybe some sort of weird distribution. But itĺs thereĺs a thereĺs a funny thing that happens with with price at the very low end low to like the 50 to 500 range. The relationship between price and in what you deliver in exchange is enormous, right? Thereĺs this expectation of the other million bonuses and you know, itĺs itĺs so outsized value relative to the cost, but the farther you go to the right and Particularly, when you go really far to the right on that axis toward the really high end, a funny thing happens that people want less and less and less stuff. And they want more and more focus. Right? So when it so you get these when youĺre in, thereĺs also another thing that goes on, especially in a, in sort of a training context or education context is that the importance of relationships of the group becomes really important too. And itĺs less about the content. So this is this is it? And I donĺt know where this observation fits in exactly. But it certainly suggests that thereĺs not a linear increase necessary in what you communicate to people. It so itĺs not like, well, if itĺs $50, if Iĺm selling something for $50, I need, you know, 500 words will do it. And if itĺs $500 than 85,000 words to do it, and then if itĺs 15,000, itĺs 50. Itĺs not that at all, it, itĺs more like if youĺre selling a 50 $50 thing. So letĺs look at financial newsletter publishers, for example, countries like the Motley Fool, and agora financial and legacy research. You know, all these these companies, the lowest their front end offer is generally a $49 newsletter. And they sell those newsletters with 36,000 word sales letters. Think about that. 36,000 words? Or letĺs letĺs call it somewhere between 20 and 30,000 20,020, to 30,000 words, sales letters to sell a $50 product, right? Itĺs shocking. But then when you eventually get up to their back end offers, that thatĺs not enough. You donĺt be you donĺt get another sales letter. Thatĺs another 30,000 words. I mean, itĺs, itĺs interesting to think about that, that it takes it takes up often a lot more to sell an offer to thatĺs lower cost to an audience than to sell a high cost offer. Thatĺs weird to think about. So getting back to Brianĺs question

what thereĺs sort of a lot here, but I think the way to think about this question is not I wouldnĺt use that framework. I think thatĺs really thatĺs a long way of the answer is no. Right? Like, itĺs not, itĺs not like those two dimensions. Right? So the dimension that Brian has, has inferred a relationship, which I donĺt think is accurate. It makes perfect sense. Itĺs a brilliant question. And itĺs really fun to think about, we can go miles deep into this question, but the relationship is inferred is that thereĺs a something changes in the structure of how we communicate with an offer with an audience, as prices increase logarithmically? And I donĺt, I donĺt think thatĺs the relationship. I think things change, but they donĺt change that way. So itĺs not a matter of like, well, Iĺve got this $50 thing. So I need to do this much. But now that Iĺm doing a 500 off dollar offer, I got to do these other things, itĺs probably itĺs probably some, itĺs somewhere in the 50 to 500 Probably have similar ish things that you need to do. And then when you get up to the $5,000 range, itĺs the selling part that becomes itĺs the the match between audience and offer and how you how you show that and demonstrate that match in do you get the connection, right, like the real emotional need, like thatĺs at the core of all marketing anyway. But like, that becomes way more important, and filtering out the wrong audience becomes way more important. And as you continue to go up the scale of pricing, I think what really begins to matter more, is not the metric of the structure of the system, or how much content you share, or what the nature of that content is, itĺs really how well do you connect the needs of that audience, which is much smaller to the value that you create, which is far more focused? I think thatĺs actually the way to think about it. Okay. That was fun. But a question. Yeah, good question. Shawn Ś 1:03:23

I hope we get to ramble or Iĺm in trouble. I think the missing the missing distinction here, which youĺve painted that already is, weĺre not, we donĺt build relationships is a separate thing. Right? Thatĺs not like, you know, someone you donĺt you donĺt frame a topic that is of interest to an audience. And then when their audience raises their hand and says, thatĺs really interesting to me, you donĺt stop and say, well, thatĺs awesome. Now, let me tell you about me. And let me tell Iĺm interested in this. And, you know, I like long walks in the woods. No, right? Thatĺs not at all what weĺre talking about. Youĺre building a relationship, in the context of the value that you create for your audience. And that caught in it begins with empathy, the reflection back on the person, that you understand the pain that they feel and the desire that they want. Oftentimes, because youĺve experienced that same pain and have that same desire, and it goes from empathy, to then credibility, gently, not necessarily a step by step thing, but all it can blend there can be MPO. Empathy and authority can overlap and credibility. They can sort of overlap one another in many ways. And oftentimes things that demonstrate empathy by an emergent property is credibility. Like if someone has felt your pain and theyĺve theyĺve done something that has helped them solve that problem that thatĺs empathy and credibility together. We just donĺt have a word for that in English language. I donĺt think that combines those Two things. But the context of the of the relationship is always, always, always framed by the ad expanded upon by the landing page. And then itĺs extended, itĺs really extended over days in those emails. So if someone raises his or her hand, because youĺre at your ad is framing your conversation, weĺll use Jonathan Baker as an example. So when Jonathan Baker who was a cycling coach for elite cyclists, who want to go faster on a bicycle, if Jonathan, were frank with sort of frames the case in his Facebook ad, and his landing page that there are really only three ways to go faster on a bicycle, these are his words, not ours. But in Jonathanĺs experience, and he has a lot of 30 plus years, he has a PhD, I believe in exercise physiology, or something very sophisticated,

tremendous amount of experience. But the context that people are entering, that are seeing the relationship building emails within the context is that they want to go faster on a bicycle. And he has explained to them that there are three ways to do that. And in heĺs going to expand upon those three ways you can make your, your bicycle lighter, you can become more aerodynamic, or you can generate more power as a cyclist. And the first two are often very expensive and very little benefit, thereĺs just not a lot of there, there, the real opportunity is in in learning how to generate more power, and having a training plan that helps you generate more power, which happens to be the thing that that Jonathan has devoted his career toward. So when when the relationship building starts, itĺs a relationship thatĺs built between is thatĺs being built between somebody who is a cyclist who wants to go faster on a bicycle, and someone who spent a career helping people go faster on a bicycle, Jonathan may introduce some other things tangentially, that contributes in sort of that relationship at the core of the relationship is built around this common interest. Thatĺs what all of the relationship buildings happens about. Itĺs not just, weĺre not saying take a step back and like, hey, letĺs get to know Jonathan Baker. Itĺs not it at all. Itĺs always in context, and always in service of the eventual offer that youĺre going to make and how that offer creates value for your audience. Because thatĺs who weĺre focusing on, someone has entered our world, because of what weĺve created a Facebook ad, we know what theyĺre interested in, we didnĺt show them some picture of a puppy and making some curiosity, then they donĺt really get to the next thing. And theyĺre like, wait a minute, this isnĺt what I was looking for. The person who clicks on the ad, in that example, is someone who is interested in going faster on a bicycle. Thatĺs the idea. Thatĺs, thatĺs what makes the ad effective. And heĺs framed the conversation that there are three ways to think about it. And he may even say the three ways to think about it and two of them, there, thereĺs very little, thereĺs a relationship between input and output on two of them is not ideal, but one of them really stands out. Shawn Ś 1:35:09

Thatĺs funny. I, I probably would say that, but that was not at all top of mind. I know Iĺve said in the past. And weĺre actually switching roles here. You said what I would say. So Iĺll say what I think you would say. Which is, will be entertaining. But I mean, I think Iĺve dealt with this before, a lot, not as much on the bridge, email side, but on things that are intellectually similar to that like, so the same kind of conversation. I think that the way to present this to the audience, we had this conversation recently, but the way to present this to your audience is that what they think of as Miss is missing an opera, what they think of as selling to everyone has consequences, right? In the consequences. Theyĺre just theyĺre making their decisions, without considering all of the consequences. And thatĺs, if we, if we all do that, then thereĺs no reason not to send everybody, right. But hereĺs the problem. Attention, the attention that our audience gives us, is not infinite, it is the most precious resource that they have. And when we show people to when we show offers to people who are not interested, we train them to stop paying attention to us. So the question I would ask your clients is, do we think itĺs a viable, intelligent, long term strategy to train our clients not to pay attention to what we send to them? Or do we think it is a better strategy in the short and long term, to train our clients to pay attention to us? Right, because the bridge email does two things beautifully, it it rewards attention, raise your hand to get something a lot more than others do. And it protects in attention. If we think about are the members of our audience have this finite amount of attention that they can give to everything in a day, a certain number of hours a day, some units of energy, we have to pay attention to things. If we are, if we send them a lot of inputs that theyĺre not interested in, we make it easier to ignore us over time. But if we send them things, and when they donĺt demonstrate interest, we donĺt keep sending them those things, unless they say they want it, then when we send them something there were theyĺre more likely to pay attention. Thatĺs how I would frame this conversation with them. And you know, at the end of the day, the other way to frame this too, and this gets released to getting better clients is positioning yourself as the expert. And you can say, I absolutely understand why that makes more sense. Like why I absolutely understand why you would think that sending to everyone all the time makes sense. Because the the it doesnĺt appear that the cost that thereĺs any cost to do that, because it doesnĺt cost more money to send more emails. But there is a cost. And in my experience, this is how I would frame it, if I were you, in my experience, there is a very significant cost. But that cost doesnĺt show up in any particular metric, or it doesnĺt show up on the balance sheet. Because of the weight, that cost is paid, where the audience can learning to stop paying attention to us. So if we send everything to everybody all the time, weĺre going to change the vast, weĺre going to train the vast majority of our audience to gradually over time, pay less and less attention until they just unsubscribe. And thatĺs not in our best interest. So I canĺt recommend to you, you and Iĺve said this a million times to clients, youĺre youĺre hiring me for my expertise and my experience. And my expertise and experience says that you will find far more value. If you send emails to people who have actively shown interest as a practice that thatĺs indisputable, because of the cost paid and attention that will cause people to gradually over time, pay less attention to us. Thatĺs how I would frame the whole conversation. And if they say, oh, thatĺs, thatĺs great, but do it anyway. Then put that person on the top of your list to be one of the clients that goes away when you get a better client. And is really a part of being in a client services business is continually upgrading your clients. Right? And at first, and Iĺve been there Iĺve worked Believe me, Iĺve worked for way longer than I want to admit with clients that were not not great clients and I banged my head against that wall more times than I can count but

Iĺm not suggesting that if you have a certain number of clients and theyĺre helping you pay the bills that you go Donĺt say you know, whatĺs my way the highway? If you donĺt want to do it this way youĺre gone. But go to the client Make your case, if the client still wants to do it a different way, put them on the list of clients to replace eventually when you get better clients. And then when youĺre when youĺre looking for new clients, look for clients where youĺre, youĺre positioning yourself as an expert for them to rely on. And then you want to attract a client that will respect your decision making. And that client gets to win when the time is right, and you will have your begin to have fewer and fewer hours. The good client, you know exactly who the good clients going to replace, eventually, and then itĺs a itĺs an easy conversation, youĺve got a new client, you can you can replace an existing client whoĺs lower quality, which we should always be doing.
